A: Keratan sulfate, which contains galactose instead of an acidic sugar. Q: What gives glycosaminoglycans their strong negative charge? A: The presence of carboxyl groups on the acidic sugars and additional sulfate groups. Q: How does the negative charge of glycosaminoglycans affect their physical structure in solution? A: The chains repel each other and are surrounded by a shell of water, causing them to stay extended. Q: What causes the "slippery" consistency of synovial fluid and mucous secretions? A: The repulsion of negative charges causes glycosaminoglycan chains to slide past one another, similar to magnets with the same polarity. Q: Why are glycosaminoglycans important for the resilience of the eye's vitreous humor and synovial fluid? A: When compressed, water is pushed out, but the repulsive negative charges cause the molecules to spring back to their original volume once pressure is removed. Q: What is a proteoglycan? A: A molecule consisting of a core protein with glycosaminoglycan chains covalently attached. Q: Which glycosaminoglycan is the only one that does not covalently attach to a protein? A: Hyaluronic acid. Q: What is the structural arrangement of a proteoglycan monomer often compared to? A: A "bottle brush," where the glycosaminoglycan chains extend outward from the central core protein. Q: How are glycosaminoglycan chains linked to the core protein in a proteoglycan? A: Typically through a trihexoside (galactose-galactose-xylose) linked via an O-glycosidic bond to a serine residue. Q: What is a proteoglycan aggregate? A: A complex formed by the noncovalent association of many proteoglycan monomers with a single molecule of hyaluronic acid. Q: Where does the synthesis of glycosaminoglycans primarily occur in the cell? A: In the Golgi apparatus. Q: What are the precursors used for the elongation of glycosaminoglycan carbohydrate chains? A: UDP-derivative sugars (nucleotide sugars). Q: Where does the sulfate for glycosaminoglycan sulfation come from? A: PAPS (3'-phosphoadenosyl-5'-phosphosulfate). Q: Where are glycosaminoglycans degraded within the cell? A: Inside lysosomes. Q: What type of enzymes are responsible for the breakdown of glycosaminoglycans? A: Acid hydrolases. Q: What is a mucopolysaccharidosis? A: A group of hereditary diseases caused by a lack of lysosomal hydrolases needed to degrade heparan sulfate or dermatan sulfate. Q: What are the general symptoms of mucopolysaccharidoses? - Skeletal deformities - Extracellular matrix abnormalities - Mental retardation - Progressive physical deterioration Q: What is the specific enzyme deficiency in Hurler syndrome (MPS I H)? A: alpha-L-iduronidase deficiency. Q: What are the clinical features of Hurler syndrome? - Corneal clouding - Intellectual disability - Stunted growth (dwarfing) - Coarse facial features - Hearing loss and upper airway obstruction - Ischemia and early death due to coronary artery deposits Q: How does Hunter syndrome (MPS II) differ from other mucopolysaccharidoses in terms of inheritance? A: It is X-linked, whereas most others are autosomal recessive. Q: What is the enzyme deficiency in Hunter syndrome? A: Iduronate sulfatase deficiency. Q: What are the primary clinical signs of Hunter syndrome? - Physical deformity - Mental retardation (ranging from mild to severe) - Absence of corneal clouding (unlike Hurler syndrome) Q: What enzyme is deficient in Sanfilippo syndrome (MPS III)? A: Any of four enzymes required to remove N-sulfated or N-acetylated glucosamine from heparan sulfate. Q: What is the primary manifestation of Sanfilippo syndrome? A: Severe nervous system disorders and mental retardation. Q: What enzyme is deficient in Sly syndrome (MPS VII)? A: Beta-glucuronidase. Q: What are the clinical signs of Sly syndrome? - Hepatosplenomegaly - Skeletal deformity - Short stature - Corneal clouding - Mental deficiency Q: What is the general structure of a collagen molecule? A: A stiff, rope-like triple helix formed by three polypeptide alpha chains wound around each other. Q: How does the organization of collagen differ in tendons versus the cornea? A: In tendons, it forms tight, parallel bundles for strength; in the cornea, it is stacked to transmit light with minimal scattering. Q: What amino acid is found at every third position in a collagen alpha chain? A: Glycine. Q: Why is glycine essential to the collagen triple helix? A: It is the smallest amino acid and fits into the tight spaces where the three chains come together. Q: What role does proline play in the structure of collagen? A: Its ring structure induces kinks that facilitate the formation of the helical conformation of alpha chains. Q: Which two amino acids are frequently found in the X and Y positions of the -Gly-X-Y- collagen sequence? A: X is often proline, and Y is often hydroxyproline or hydroxylysine. Q: What is the purpose of hydroxyproline in collagen? A: It stabilizes the triple helix by maximizing the formation of interchain hydrogen bonds. Q: What are Type I, II, and III collagens classified as? A: Fibril-forming collagens. Q: Where is Type I collagen typically found? A: In high-tensile strength structures like skin, bone, tendons, and the cornea. Q: Where is Type II collagen restricted to? A: Cartilaginous structures. Q: Where is Type III collagen most prevalent? A: In distensible tissues such as blood vessels. Q: What is the function of Type IV collagen? A: It forms a three-dimensional mesh that acts as a major component of basement membranes. Q: Where are the polypeptide precursors of collagen (prepro-alpha chains) synthesized? A: On ribosomes on the rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER). Q: What are the requirements for the enzymes prolyl hydroxylase and lysyl hydroxylase to function? - Molecular oxygen - Ferrous iron (Fe2+) - Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) as a reducing agent Q: What is the clinical result of Vitamin C deficiency regarding collagen? A: Scurvy, characterized by impaired triple helix stability, decreased tensile strength of fibers, and capillary fragility leading to bruising. Q: What happens during the glycosylation step of collagen synthesis? A: Glucose and galactose are sequentially attached to hydroxylysine residues. Q: What is procollagen? A: A collagen precursor containing a central triple helix flanked by nonhelical terminal extensions called propeptides. Q: What enzyme is responsible for the cross-linking of collagen fibrils? A: Lysyl oxidase (a copper-containing enzyme). Q: How does lysyl oxidase facilitate collagen cross-linking? A: It oxidatively deaminates lysyl and hydroxylysyl residues to form reactive aldehydes (allysine), which condense to form covalent cross-links. Q: What is Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (EDS)? A: A group of connective tissue disorders caused by defects in fibrillar collagen metabolism, processing enzymes, or amino acid sequences. Q: What are the signs of the classic form of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome? - Fragile, stretchy skin - Loose (hypermobile) joints Q: Which collagen type mutation is associated with potentially lethal vascular problems in EDS? A: Type III collagen. Q: What is Osteogenesis Imperfecta (OI), also known as "brittle bone syndrome"? A: A group of inherited disorders where bones easily bend and fracture due to defects in Type I collagen. Q: What is the difference between Osteogenesis Imperfecta Tarda (Type I) and Congenita (Type II)? A: Type I presents with fractures in infancy but is less severe; Type II is the most severe, often resulting in death in utero or the neonatal period due to pulmonary hypoplasia. Q: What is the characteristic amino acid substitution often found in severe Osteogenesis Imperfecta? A: Glycine residues are replaced by amino acids with bulky side chains, preventing proper triple helix folding. Q: What is the primary functional difference between collagen and elastin? A: Collagen provides high tensile strength and toughness, whereas elastin has rubber-like properties that allow tissues to stretch and recoil. Q: Where are elastic fibers commonly found? - Lungs - Walls of large arteries - Elastic ligaments Q: What is the precursor of elastin? A: Tropoelastin, a linear polypeptide made of small, nonpolar amino acids like glycine, alanine, and valine. Q: What glycoprotein acts as a scaffold for the deposition of tropoelastin? A: Fibrillin. Q: What is the desmosine cross-link in elastin? A: A structure formed by three allysyl side chains and one unaltered lysyl side chain, creating an interconnected rubbery network. Q: What disorder is caused by mutations in the fibrillin-1 protein? A: Marfan syndrome. Q: What are the clinical features of Marfan syndrome? - Impaired structural integrity of the skeleton - Eye abnormalities - Cardiovascular system defects Q: Why might patients with Osteogenesis Imperfecta or Marfan syndrome have blue sclera? A: Tissue thinning allows the underlying pigment of the eye to show through. Q: What is the physiological role of alpha-1 antitrypsin (A1AT)? A: It inhibits proteolytic enzymes, most importantly neutrophil elastase, to prevent the destruction of elastin in alveolar walls. Q: Where is alpha-1 antitrypsin primarily synthesized? A: In the liver. Q: What disease results from the destruction of alveolar connective tissue due to A1AT deficiency? A: Emphysema. Q: How does smoking exacerbate alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency? A: It oxidizes a specific methionine residue on A1AT, inactivating the inhibitor and allowing elastase to destroy lung tissue unopposed. Q: What can happen to the liver in some forms of alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency? A: The mutated protein polymerizes in the endoplasmic reticulum of hepatocytes, leading to liver scarring or cirrhosis. Q: How can Vitamin C deficiency be diagnosed based on physical signs? A: Presence of bruises on limbs due to blood leakage from fragile capillaries (subcutaneous extravasation). Q: What is the active form of Vitamin C mentioned in the text? A: Ascorbic acid (specifically L-ascorbic acid). Q: Why is Vitamin C an essential nutrient for humans but not for most other mammals? A: Humans lack the enzyme L-gulonolactone oxidase, which is required to synthesize ascorbic acid from glucuronic acid. Q: What is the function of Vitamin C in the uronic acid pathway? A: In humans, it has no biosynthetic function in this pathway as it cannot be made; it must be obtained from the diet. Q: How does Vitamin C facilitate the stabilization of collagen? A: It acts as a reducing agent for prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase, which form the hydroxyproline and hydroxylysine necessary for interchain cross-linking and triple helix stability.

topics.topic: Identify the defects in collagen synthesis based on given data (Ostegenesis Imperfecta)
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: What is the clinical name for the inherited condition known as "brittle bone syndrome," which is defined by bones that fracture or bend with ease?
A: Osteogenesis imperfecta (OI).

Q: Besides bone fragility, what are two common physical features associated with the progression of osteogenesis imperfecta?
A: 
- A rotated and twisted spine (kyphotic or "humped-back" appearance)
- Delayed healing of wounds

Q: What characterizes the onset and clinical presentation of Type I osteogenesis imperfecta, also known as osteogenesis imperfecta tarda?
A: It appears in early infancy, manifesting as bone fractures following minimal physical trauma.

Q: What specific defect in collagen chain synthesis causes Type I osteogenesis imperfecta (OI tarda)?
A: A reduction in the overall production of alpha-1 and alpha-2 chains.

Q: How might a clinician suspect osteogenesis imperfecta before a child is born?
A: By using prenatal ultrasound to detect fractures or bowing of the long bones in the fetus.

Q: Which form of osteogenesis imperfecta is considered the most severe, and when does death typically occur for these patients?
A: Type II (osteogenesis imperfecta congenita); death usually occurs either in the womb or during the neonatal period.

Q: What is the primary medical cause of death for infants born with Type II osteogenesis imperfecta?
A: Pulmonary hypoplasia.

Q: Most patients suffering from severe osteogenesis imperfecta have genetic mutations affecting which specific collagen precursors?
A: The pro-alpha-1 or pro-alpha-2 chains of Type I collagen.

Q: What is the most frequent molecular mutation observed in the -Gly-X-Y- sequence of patients with severe osteogenesis imperfecta?
A: The replacement of glycine residues with amino acids that possess bulky side chains.

Q: How do mutations involving glycine substitutions specifically interfere with the assembly of collagen fibers in osteogenesis imperfecta?
A: They result in structurally abnormal pro-alpha chains that prevent the collagen from folding into its required triple-helical shape.

Q: Why do some patients with osteogenesis imperfecta exhibit blue sclera in their eyes?
A: The ocular tissue becomes thin, which allows the pigment from underlying structures to show through.

topics.topic: Interpret genetic basis of Duchene muscular dystrophy
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: Which specific protein is encoded by the gene that serves as the genetic basis for Duchenne muscular dystrophy?
A: Dystrophin.

Q: Mutations in the gene for dystrophin are responsible for which two types of muscular dystrophy?
A: 
- Duchenne muscular dystrophy
- Becker muscular dystrophy

Q: Besides muscular dystrophies, what cardiac condition is associated with mutations in the dystrophin gene?
A: Dilated cardiomyopathy.

Q: What is the primary structural function of the dystrophin protein within a muscle cell?
A: It acts as a bridge that connects the internal actin cytoskeleton to the external extracellular matrix at the plasma membrane's inner face.

Q: Why is the connection between the actin cytoskeleton and the extracellular matrix critical for muscle cells?
A: This physical link is a requirement for the successful assembly of synaptic junctions.

Q: What is the cellular mechanism that leads to the development of Duchenne muscular dystrophy?
A: The disease is caused by the presence of mutated dystrophin proteins that are incapable of supporting the creation of effective synaptic junctions.

Q: Which proteins, in addition to dystrophin, are identified as parts of the muscle cell's contractile apparatus?
A: 
- Titin
- Nebulin
- Alpha-actinin
- Desmin

Q: What is the biological significance of titin in the context of muscle structure?
A: It is recognized as the largest known protein in the world and functions to anchor the ends of myofibrils.

Q: Mutations in which enzymes can lead to congenital forms of limb-girdle muscular dystrophy?
A: Glycosyltransferases that are responsible for modifying alpha-dystroglycan.

Q: Deficiencies or mutations in which protein group, other than dystrophin or glycosyltransferases, are linked to limb-girdle muscular dystrophy?
A: The polypeptide components of the sarcoglycan complex.

Q: According to the text, which proteins are implicated in causing dilated cardiomyopathy?
A: 
- Dystrophin
- Muscle LIM protein
- CREB (cyclic AMP response-element binding protein)
- Desmin
- Lamin

Q: What are the specific roles of muscle LIM protein and CREB in cardiac muscle cells?
A: Muscle LIM protein assists in organizing the contractile machinery, while CREB controls the expression of various internal genes.

topics.topic: Explain the transport and uptake of glucose in cells, steps of glycolysis and citric acid cycle along with enzymes, co enzymes and cofactors involved
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: What is the primary reason glucose requires specialized transport systems to enter cells?
A: Glucose is unable to move across cell membranes via simple diffusion.

Q: What are the two main types of transport systems used for glucose uptake in the body?
A: - A system that is independent of both sodium and ATP (passive facilitated diffusion)
- A system that is dependent on both sodium and ATP (secondary active transport)

Q: How many isoforms of glucose transporters (GLUT) are found in the sodium-independent transport system?
A: There are 14 known isoforms, labeled GLUT-1 through GLUT-14.

Q: By what mechanism do GLUT proteins move glucose across the cell membrane?
A: They utilize facilitated diffusion, acting as uniporters that transport one molecule at a time by switching between two different conformational states.

Q: Which glucose transporter is primarily responsible for uptake in neurons?
A: GLUT-3.

Q: In which tissues is GLUT-4 most abundant?
A: Adipose tissue and skeletal muscle.

Q: How does insulin affect glucose uptake in muscle and fat cells?
A: It increases the number of GLUT-4 transporters active on the cell membrane.

Q: Which glucose transporter is found in the liver, kidneys, and pancreatic beta cells?
A: GLUT-2.

Q: What unique functional capability does GLUT-2 possess regarding the direction of transport?
A: It can transport glucose into cells when blood sugar is high or out of cells (such as from the liver) when blood sugar levels are low during fasting.

Q: What is the primary function of GLUT-5, and where is it located?
A: It is a specialized transporter for fructose located in the small intestine and the testes.

Q: Where does the sodium-dependent glucose cotransport system (SGLT) primarily occur?
A: It occurs in the epithelial cells of the intestine, the renal tubules, and the choroid plexus.

Q: How is the transport of glucose against its concentration gradient powered in the SGLT system?
A: It is powered by the electrochemical gradient of sodium. Sodium moves down its gradient into the cell, which pulls glucose "uphill" into the cell.

Q: Why is the SGLT system considered to require ATP indirectly?
A: ATP is needed by the Na+–K+ ATPase pump to maintain the high extracellular sodium concentration that drives the cotransport process.

Q: What are the two functional stages of the glycolytic pathway?
A: - The energy-investment phase (the first five reactions)
- The energy-generation phase (the remaining reactions)

Q: What happens during the energy-investment phase of glycolysis?
A: Phosphorylated forms of metabolic intermediates are created at the cost of two ATP molecules.

Q: What is the net yield of ATP and NADH from the metabolism of one glucose molecule in aerobic glycolysis?
A: A net of two ATP and two NADH molecules.

Q: What is the purpose of phosphorylating glucose as it enters the cell?
A: It effectively traps the sugar inside the cytosol as glucose 6-phosphate because phosphorylated sugars cannot cross the cell membrane.

Q: Which enzymes catalyze the initial phosphorylation of glucose?
A: Hexokinase (found in most tissues) and glucokinase (Hexokinase IV, found in the liver and pancreatic beta cells).

Q: What cofactor is required by kinases like hexokinase during glucose phosphorylation?
A: Magnesium (Mg2+), which complexes with ATP.

Q: How does the Michaelis constant (Km) of hexokinase I–III compare to that of glucokinase?
A: Hexokinase has a low Km (high affinity) for glucose, while glucokinase has a much higher Km (lower affinity).

Q: How does the maximal velocity (Vmax) of glucokinase differ from other hexokinases?
A: Glucokinase has a high Vmax, which allows the liver to rapidly process large amounts of glucose following a carbohydrate-rich meal.

Q: What protein regulates glucokinase activity in the liver through reversible binding?
A: Glucokinase regulatory protein (GKRP).

Q: Which enzyme catalyzes the isomerization of glucose 6-phosphate to fructose 6-phosphate?
A: Phosphoglucose isomerase.

Q: What is considered the most important control point and rate-limiting step of glycolysis?
A: The phosphorylation of fructose 6-phosphate to fructose 1,6-bisphosphate by the enzyme phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1).

Q: What are the primary allosteric inhibitors of PFK-1?
A: ATP and citrate.

Q: What molecule is the most potent allosteric activator of PFK-1?
A: Fructose 2,6-bisphosphate.

Q: Which enzyme is responsible for the cleavage of fructose 1,6-bisphosphate into two triose phosphates?
A: Aldolase.

Q: Name the two products formed by the action of Aldolase in glycolysis.
A: Dihydroxyacetone phosphate (DHAP) and glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate.

Q: What enzyme facilitates the interconversion of DHAP and glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate?
A: Triose phosphate isomerase.

Q: What is the first oxidation-reduction reaction in glycolysis?
A: The oxidation of glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate to 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate (1,3-BPG) by glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate dehydrogenase.

Q: What coenzyme is reduced during the oxidation of glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate?
A: NAD+ is reduced to NADH.

Q: How does pentavalent arsenic (arsenate) interfere with glycolysis?
A: It competes with inorganic phosphate as a substrate for glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate dehydrogenase, resulting in a product that bypasses ATP synthesis.

Q: Which glycolytic enzyme catalyzes the first substrate-level phosphorylation to produce ATP?
A: Phosphoglycerate kinase.

Q: What reaction does enolase catalyze in glycolysis?
A: The dehydration of 2-phosphoglycerate to phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP).

Q: What substance can inhibit the enzyme enolase?
A: Fluoride.

Q: Which enzyme catalyzes the final irreversible reaction of glycolysis?
A: Pyruvate kinase (PK).

Q: How is hepatic pyruvate kinase regulated covalently?
A: It is inactivated by phosphorylation via cAMP-dependent protein kinase A (when glucagon is high) and reactivated by dephosphorylation.

Q: What are the two major fates of NADH produced during glycolysis?
A: - In aerobic conditions, it is reoxidized by the electron transport chain.
- In anaerobic conditions, it is reoxidized by reducing pyruvate to lactate.

Q: What enzyme reduces pyruvate to lactate in anaerobic glycolysis?
A: Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH).

Q: Which tissues rely on anaerobic glycolysis for ATP production?
A: Tissues that lack mitochondria (like red blood cells) or those with poor oxygen supply (like the cornea and lens of the eye).

Q: What is the end product of aerobic glycolysis?
A: Pyruvate.

Q: Into which cellular compartment is pyruvate transported for further oxidation?
A: The mitochondrial matrix.

Q: Which multienzyme complex converts pyruvate into acetyl CoA?
A: The pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDHC).

Q: Name the three catalytic enzymes that make up the PDH complex.
A: - Pyruvate decarboxylase (E1)
- Dihydrolipoyl transacetylase (E2)
- Dihydrolipoyl dehydrogenase (E3)

Q: What are the five coenzymes required by the PDH complex?
A: - Thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP)
- Lipoic acid
- Coenzyme A (CoA)
- Flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD)
- Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD+)

Q: How does arsenic poisoning affect the PDH complex?
A: Arsenite (the trivalent form) binds to the thiol groups of lipoic acid, making it unavailable as a coenzyme.

Q: How is the PDH complex regulated by covalent modification?
A: PDH kinase phosphorylates and inhibits E1, while PDH phosphatase dephosphorylates and activates E1.

Q: What ions activate PDH phosphatase to stimulate energy production in skeletal muscle?
A: Calcium ions (Ca2+).

Q: Where do the reactions of the Citric Acid Cycle (TCA cycle) take place?
A: Entirely within the mitochondria.

Q: What enzyme catalyzes the initial condensation of acetyl CoA and oxaloacetate (OAA)?
A: Citrate synthase.

Q: Which TCA cycle enzyme catalyzes the isomerization of citrate to isocitrate?
A: Aconitase.

Q: What metal-sulfur component is found in the enzyme aconitase?
A: It is an iron-sulfur (Fe-S) protein.

Q: Which enzyme catalyzes the first oxidative decarboxylation in the TCA cycle?
A: Isocitrate dehydrogenase.

Q: What are the products of the reaction catalyzed by isocitrate dehydrogenase?
A: Alpha-ketoglutarate, CO2, and NADH.

Q: Which TCA cycle enzyme complex is structurally and functionally similar to the PDH complex?
A: The alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase complex.

Q: What are the coenzymes required by the alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase complex?
A: TPP, lipoic acid, FAD, NAD+, and CoA.

Q: Which TCA cycle reaction produces a molecule of GTP via substrate-level phosphorylation?
A: The cleavage of succinyl CoA to succinate by succinate thiokinase (succinyl CoA synthetase).

Q: Which TCA cycle enzyme is embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane and functions as Complex II of the ETC?
A: Succinate dehydrogenase.

Q: What coenzyme is reduced during the oxidation of succinate to fumarate?
A: FAD is reduced to FADH2.

Q: Which enzyme catalyzes the hydration of fumarate to malate?
A: Fumarase (fumarate hydratase).

Q: What is the final reaction of the Citric Acid Cycle?
A: The oxidation of malate to oxaloacetate by malate dehydrogenase, which produces the third NADH of the cycle.

Q: What is the total yield of high-energy electron carriers and GTP from one turn of the TCA cycle (one acetyl CoA)?
A: Three NADH, one FADH2, and one GTP.

Q: Approximately how many ATP molecules are produced from the complete oxidation of one molecule of pyruvate?
A: A total of 15 ATP (12 from the TCA cycle per acetyl CoA, plus 3 from the NADH produced by PDHC).

Q: Which three TCA cycle enzymes are the primary sites of regulation?
A: Citrate synthase, isocitrate dehydrogenase, and the alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase complex.

Q: How do ADP and ATP levels regulate the Citric Acid Cycle?
A: High ADP (low energy) activates the cycle, while high ATP (high energy) inhibits key enzymes of the cycle.

learningObjectives.objective: MS-B-004. Glycolysis and Tricarboxylic acid cycle (TCA)

topics.topic: Discuss the provision of energy to the muscles and cells through glycolytic pathway and TCA cycle
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: How is the term metabolism defined in a biological context?
A: It is the total of all chemical transformations that take place within a cell, a tissue, or the entire organism.

Q: What are the two primary classifications for metabolic pathways?
A: They are categorized as either catabolic, which involves degradation, or anabolic, which involves synthesis.

Q: What is the function of catabolic pathways regarding energy?
A: These pathways break down energy-dense fuel molecules to capture chemical energy in the form of ATP.

Q: How does the role of anabolic pathways differ from catabolic pathways?
A: Anabolic pathways use energy, usually from ATP hydrolysis, to build complex end products from simple starting materials.

Q: Which central metabolic pathway is utilized by all tissues to break down glucose for energy?
A: The glycolytic pathway.

Q: What are the two potential end products of glycolysis depending on oxygen availability?
A: The process ends in pyruvate when oxygen is present (aerobic) or lactate when oxygen is absent or insufficient (anaerobic).

Q: Where does the glycolytic pathway take place within the cell?
A: It occurs in the cytosol.

Q: What are the two functional stages of the glycolytic pathway?
A: The first stage is the energy-investment phase, and the second is the energy-generation phase.

Q: How many ATP molecules are used during the energy-investment phase of glycolysis?
A: Two molecules of ATP are consumed to synthesize phosphorylated intermediates.

Q: What is the net energy yield of anaerobic glycolysis per molecule of glucose?
A: Two molecules of ATP are produced with no net change in NADH levels.

Q: What is the net energy yield of aerobic glycolysis per molecule of glucose?
A: It results in two molecules of ATP and two molecules of NADH.

Q: Which enzyme acts as the most significant control point and the rate-limiting step of glycolysis?
A: Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1).

Q: How do high levels of ATP and citrate regulate PFK-1?
A: They act as allosteric inhibitors, slowing down the pathway when energy or intermediates are abundant.

Q: What is the effect of high AMP levels on PFK-1?
A: AMP acts as an allosteric activator, signaling that the cell’s energy stores are low and need replenishment.

Q: What role does fructose 2,6-bisphosphate play in glycolysis?
A: It is the most powerful activator of PFK-1 and can trigger the enzyme even when ATP levels are high.

Q: How does insulin influence the rate of glycolysis in the liver?
A: It increases the synthesis of key enzymes—glucokinase, phosphofructokinase, and pyruvate kinase—thereby speeding up the conversion of glucose to pyruvate.

Q: Which glucose transporter is specifically responsible for insulin-dependent glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue?
A: GLUT-4.

Q: What happens to the number of active GLUT-4 transporters in muscle tissue when insulin is present?
A: The number of active transporters increases to facilitate higher glucose uptake.

Q: Why must glucose be phosphorylated upon entering a cell?
A: Phosphorylation traps the sugar inside the cell as cytosolic glucose 6-phosphate because phosphorylated molecules cannot easily cross the cell membrane.

Q: Which enzyme catalyzes the third irreversible reaction of glycolysis, resulting in the production of ATP and pyruvate?
A: Pyruvate kinase (PK).

Q: How is hepatic pyruvate kinase regulated when blood glucose is low?
A: Glucagon increases cAMP levels, activating protein kinase A to phosphorylate and inactivate the enzyme, which slows glycolysis.

Q: What causes the accumulation of lactate in exercising skeletal muscle?
A: When NADH production exceeds the oxidative capacity of the electron transport chain, the ratio of NADH to NAD+ rises, favoring the reduction of pyruvate to lactate.

Q: What is the primary fate of pyruvate in tissues that lack mitochondria or have poor oxygen supply?
A: It is reduced to lactate via the enzyme lactate dehydrogenase.

Q: Which multienzyme complex is responsible for converting pyruvate into acetyl CoA?
A: The pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDHC).

Q: Where is the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex located?
A: Within the mitochondrial matrix.

Q: What five coenzymes are required for the functioning of the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex?
A: - Thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP)
- Lipoic acid
- Coenzyme A (CoA)
- Flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD)
- Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD+)

Q: How does calcium influence energy production in skeletal muscle through the PDHC?
A: Calcium release during muscle contraction activates PDH phosphatase, which activates the PDHC to produce more acetyl CoA for energy.

Q: What are the three high-energy products that allosterically inhibit the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex?
A: ATP, acetyl CoA, and NADH.

Q: What is the major source of acetyl CoA for the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle?
A: The oxidative decarboxylation of pyruvate by the PDHC.

Q: Why is the TCA cycle considered an aerobic pathway?
A: It requires oxygen as the final electron acceptor in the electron transport chain to oxidize the reduced coenzymes it produces.

Q: Where do all the reactions of the TCA cycle occur?
A: In the mitochondria.

Q: What are the two alternate names for the TCA cycle?
A: The Krebs cycle or the citric acid cycle.

Q: What is the first reaction of the TCA cycle?
A: The condensation of acetyl CoA with oxaloacetate to form citrate, catalyzed by citrate synthase.

Q: Which TCA cycle enzyme catalyzes the irreversible oxidative decarboxylation that produces the first NADH and CO2?
A: Isocitrate dehydrogenase.

Q: How does ADP affect the rate of the TCA cycle?
A: It acts as an allosteric activator of isocitrate dehydrogenase, signaling a low-energy state and increasing cycle activity.

Q: Which enzyme in the TCA cycle catalyzes a reaction that is virtually identical in mechanism to the PDHC?
A: The alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase complex.

Q: Which TCA cycle reaction results in the production of GTP through substrate-level phosphorylation?
A: The cleavage of succinyl CoA to succinate by succinate thiokinase.

Q: Which TCA cycle enzyme is uniquely embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane?
A: Succinate dehydrogenase.

Q: How many pairs of electrons are transferred to coenzymes during one turn of the TCA cycle?
A: Four pairs: three reduce NAD+ to NADH and one reduces FAD to FADH2.

Q: What is the total yield of ATP from the oxidation of a single acetyl CoA molecule?
A: 12 ATP are produced via substrate-level and oxidative phosphorylation.

Q: What is the total ATP yield from the complete mitochondrial oxidation of one molecule of pyruvate?
A: 15 ATP.

Q: Which three enzymes of the TCA cycle are the primary sites of regulation?
A: Citrate synthase, isocitrate dehydrogenase, and the alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase complex.

Q: What is the overall purpose of the TCA cycle regarding fuel molecules?
A: It is the final common pathway for the oxidative catabolism of carbohydrates, fats, and amino acids, converting their carbon skeletons into CO2 and energy.

Q: How does the cell ensure that the TCA cycle and oxidative phosphorylation are coordinated?
A: Both processes are upregulated when there is an increase in the ADP to ATP ratio, indicating a need for more energy.

Q: What is the function of creatine phosphate in muscle cells?
A: It acts as a rapidly mobilized reserve of high-energy phosphates that can be transferred to ADP to maintain ATP levels during the start of intense contraction.

Q: Which enzyme is responsible for the reversible phosphorylation of creatine?
A: Creatine kinase.

learningObjectives.objective: MS-B-005. Protein Digestion & Transport across cell

topics.topic: Describe the digestion and absorption of proteins
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: What is the typical amount of protein consumed daily in the American diet?
A: Between 70 and 100 grams.

Q: Why must dietary proteins be chemically broken down before they can be utilized by the body?
A: They are generally too large to be absorbed directly by the intestine.

Q: What is a notable exception where whole proteins are absorbed by the body?
A: Newborns can absorb maternal antibodies from breast milk.

Q: Which three organs produce the proteolytic enzymes required for protein degradation?
A: - The stomach
- The pancreas
- The small intestine

Q: What are the three final products of protein digestion that the body can absorb?
A: - Free amino acids
- Dipeptides
- Tripeptides

Q: What are the two primary components of gastric juice in the stomach?
A: Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and the proenzyme known as pepsinogen.

Q: Which specific stomach cells secrete hydrochloric acid?
A: Parietal cells.

Q: What are the two main functions of hydrochloric acid in the stomach?
A: - It kills certain bacteria.
- It denatures proteins, which makes them easier for proteases to hydrolyze.

Q: What is the approximate pH level of stomach acid?
A: Between 2 and 3.

Q: Which stomach cells are responsible for the secretion of pepsinogen?
A: Chief cells.

Q: What is a zymogen?
A: An inactive enzyme precursor that contains extra amino acids that prevent catalytic activity until they are removed.

Q: How is the zymogen pepsinogen transformed into its active form?
A: It is activated by hydrochloric acid or through autocatalysis by previously activated pepsin molecules.

Q: What type of protease is pepsin?
A: An acid-stable endopeptidase.

Q: What products does pepsin release from dietary proteins?
A: Peptides and a limited number of free amino acids.

Q: Which two hormones trigger the release and activation of pancreatic zymogens?
A: Secretin and cholecystokinin.

Q: Where is enteropeptidase located, and what is its role in protein digestion?
A: It is found on the luminal brush border of intestinal mucosal cells. It converts the zymogen trypsinogen into active trypsin.

Q: Why is trypsin considered a critical common activator in the pancreas?
A: Once activated, it goes on to convert all other pancreatic zymogens into their active enzyme forms.

Q: Which three pancreatic proteases are classified as serine endopeptidases?
A: - Trypsin
- Chymotrypsin
- Elastase

Q: Which two pancreatic proteases are categorized as exopeptidases?
A: Carboxypeptidase A and Carboxypeptidase B.

Q: What is the cleavage specificity of trypsin?
A: It cleaves peptide bonds where the carbonyl group is donated by arginine or lysine.

Q: What is the cleavage specificity of chymotrypsin?
A: It targets peptide bonds where the carbonyl group comes from aromatic or bulky hydrophobic amino acids, specifically tryptophan, tyrosine, phenylalanine, methionine, or leucine.

Q: What is the cleavage specificity of elastase?
A: It cleaves bonds adjacent to small amino acids such as alanine, glycine, or serine.

Q: What is the function of aminopeptidase in the small intestine?
A: It is an exopeptidase that repeatedly removes the N-terminal amino acid from oligopeptides to produce smaller peptides and free amino acids.

Q: How are free amino acids transported from the intestinal lumen into the enterocytes?
A: Through a secondary active transport system on the apical membrane that is linked to sodium (Na+).

Q: By what mechanism are dipeptides and tripeptides absorbed into enterocytes?
A: Through a transport system linked to hydrogen ions (H+).

Q: What happens to dipeptides and tripeptides after they are absorbed into the enterocyte cytosol?
A: They are hydrolyzed into individual free amino acids.

Q: How do free amino acids move from the enterocytes into the portal blood system?
A: Through facilitated diffusion.

Q: Why are only free amino acids found in the portal vein after a protein-rich meal?
A: Because di- and tripeptides are hydrolyzed into individual amino acids within the enterocyte before entering the blood.

Q: What are the two possible fates of amino acids once they reach the portal system?
A: They are either metabolized in the liver or released into general circulation.

Q: Which specific group of amino acids bypasses liver metabolism and is primarily sent to the muscles?
A: Branched-chain amino acids.

Q: What are the clinical consequences of a deficiency in pancreatic secretions?
A: Incomplete digestion of protein and fat, resulting in undigested protein and lipids (steatorrhea) appearing in the feces.

Q: What is the cause of Celiac disease?
A: Immune-mediated damage to the small intestine triggered by the ingestion of gluten (specifically gliadin).

Q: What is the biochemical cause of the inherited disorder cystinuria?
A: A defect in the transport system responsible for the uptake of cystine and the dibasic amino acids lysine, arginine, and ornithine (COAL).

Q: Where is the transport defect in cystinuria located?
A: In the small intestine and the proximal tubule of the kidney.

Q: What is the primary clinical manifestation of cystinuria?
A: The formation of cystine kidney stones (calculi) that can block the urinary tract.

Q: How is cystinuria typically treated?
A: Through oral hydration.

Q: What drives the transport of amino acids from the low-concentration extracellular fluid into the high-concentration interior of cells?
A: Active transport systems that are powered by the hydrolysis of ATP.

Q: What clinical symptoms are associated with Hartnup disorder?
A: Dermatologic and neurologic symptoms that resemble pellagra, caused by defects in the transport of tryptophan and other neutral amino acids.

topics.topic: Discuss the uptake of amino acids by cells
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: Why must dietary proteins undergo hydrolysis before the body can absorb them?
A: Most proteins are too large to be taken up by the intestinal tract and must be broken down into individual amino acids or small di- and tripeptides to be absorbed.

Q: What is a specific instance where whole proteins can be absorbed by the intestine without being broken down?
A: Infants can take up intact antibodies from their mother's breast milk.

Q: By what mechanism are free amino acids absorbed into enterocytes at the apical membrane?
A: They enter the cells through a secondary transport system that is dependent on sodium (Na+).

Q: How do small peptides like di- and tripeptides enter the intestinal cells?
A: They are taken up via a transport system linked to hydrogen ions (H+).

Q: What is the fate of di- and tripeptides once they enter the cytosol of the enterocyte?
A: They are broken down into free amino acids by hydrolysis.

Q: How do amino acids move from the enterocytes into the portal blood system?
A: They are released through the cell membrane via facilitated diffusion.

Q: What form of protein subunits are found in the portal vein after eating a protein-rich meal?
A: Only free amino acids are present.

Q: How does the level of free amino acids in the extracellular fluid compare to the level inside the cells?
A: The concentration is notably lower in the extracellular fluids than within the cells.

Q: What is required for amino acids to move from the extracellular environment into the cells against their concentration gradient?
A: Active transport systems that are powered by the hydrolysis of ATP.

Q: How many transport systems exist for moving amino acids into cells?
A: There are at least seven different systems, and they have overlapping specificities for various amino acids.

Q: Which two physiological structures utilize the same systems for transporting amino acids?
A: The proximal tubule of the kidney and the small intestine.

Q: What happens if an individual has a genetic defect in one of the amino acid transport systems shared by the gut and the kidneys?
A: They will be unable to absorb certain amino acids in both the intestine and the renal tubules.

Q: What is the specific transport defect found in the condition known as cystinuria?
A: A failure in the carrier system responsible for the uptake of cystine and the dibasic amino acids ornithine, arginine, and lysine.

Q: Which mnemonic is used to remember the four amino acids affected by the transport defect in cystinuria?
A: COAL (Cystine, Ornithine, Arginine, and Lysine).

Q: What is the clinical significance of cystinuria in terms of its frequency?
A: It occurs in 1 out of 7,000 people and is the most common genetic mistake related to amino acid transport.

Q: What is a major clinical complication of cystinuria?
A: The development of kidney stones (calculi) caused by the precipitation of cystine, which can lead to urinary tract obstructions.

Q: What is the recommended primary treatment to help manage cystinuria?
A: Maintaining high levels of oral hydration.

Q: What causes the symptoms associated with Hartnup disorder?
A: Defects in the transport of neutral amino acids, specifically tryptophan.

Q: Following absorption, what is the primary destination for most amino acids in the portal blood?
A: They are either processed by the liver or distributed to the general circulation.

Q: Which group of amino acids is specifically sent from the liver to the muscles rather than being metabolized by the liver first?
A: Branched-chain amino acids.

topics.topic: Define amino acid pool and nitrogen balance.
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: What is the conceptual definition of the amino acid pool?
A: It is the total collection of free amino acids found across the entire body, specifically within cells, the blood, and extracellular fluids, treated as a single collective entity.

Q: Which three processes serve as the primary sources for the body's amino acid pool?
A: 
- The breakdown of existing proteins within the body
- The intake of protein through the diet
- The internal synthesis of nonessential amino acids from metabolic intermediates

Q: In what three ways is the amino acid pool depleted?
A: 
- The creation of new body proteins
- The use of amino acids as building blocks for vital nitrogen-containing small molecules
- The chemical conversion of amino acids into energy-related molecules like glucose, glycogen, fatty acids, ketone bodies, or carbon dioxide and water

Q: Approximately how many grams of amino acids make up the amino acid pool in a standard adult?
A: The pool consists of roughly 90 to 100 grams of amino acids.

Q: How does the size of the amino acid pool compare to the total mass of protein in a 70-kg man?
A: The pool is very small (about 90–100 g) compared to the total body protein, which is approximately 12 kg.

Q: What is the definition of nitrogen balance in a healthy, well-nourished person?
A: It is a state where the intake of amino acids into the body's pool is perfectly matched by the output, maintaining a constant amount of amino acids.

Q: What metabolic term describes the amino acid pool when the rates of input and output are equal?
A: The pool is considered to be in a steady state.

Q: Where is the amino acid pool positioned in the conceptual framework of whole-body nitrogen metabolism?
A: It is located at the center of the body's nitrogen metabolic processes.

Q: What is the primary form in which nitrogen enters the human body?
A: It enters mostly as amino acids found in dietary protein.

Q: What are the primary routes by which nitrogen is removed from the body?
A: It is eliminated as urea, ammonia, and various other byproducts of amino acid metabolism.

Q: What occurs to amino acids that are present in quantities exceeding the cell's immediate biosynthetic requirements?
A: These excess amino acids are not stored and are instead rapidly broken down.

Q: What characterizes the first phase of amino acid breakdown?
A: It involves extracting the alpha-amino groups to produce ammonia and alpha-keto acids, which are the carbon skeletons of the amino acids.

Q: What is the most significant pathway for the body to eliminate nitrogen?
A: The synthesis of urea.

topics.topic: Explain ATP -dependent & ATP independent systems of protein degradation.
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: What are the two primary enzyme systems used for the breakdown of unneeded or damaged proteins?
A: - The ATP-dependent ubiquitin-proteasome system of the cytosol
- The ATP-independent degradative system of the lysosomes

Q: Which location in the cell houses the ATP-dependent protein degradation system?
A: The cytosol.

Q: Which location in the cell houses the ATP-independent protein degradation system?
A: The lysosomes.

Q: What specific types of proteins are primarily targeted by the ubiquitin-proteasome system?
A: Endogenous proteins, which are those synthesized within the cell.

Q: What specific types of proteins are primarily targeted by the lysosomal degradative system?
A: - Extracellular proteins (such as plasma proteins) taken up via endocytosis
- Cell-surface membrane proteins taken up via receptor-mediated endocytosis

Q: What is ubiquitin and what is its function in protein degradation?
A: Ubiquitin is a small, globular, non-enzymatic protein that covalently attaches to target proteins to mark them for degradation.

Q: Describe the chemical bond formed during the ubiquitination of a protein substrate.
A: The alpha-carboxyl group of ubiquitin's C-terminal glycine links to the epsilon-amino group of a lysine residue on the protein being targeted.

Q: How many steps are required for the covalent attachment of ubiquitin to a protein, and does it require energy?
A: It is a three-step enzyme-catalyzed process that requires energy in the form of ATP.

Q: What is a polyubiquitin chain?
A: A chain created by the consecutive addition of multiple ubiquitin molecules to a protein substrate.

Q: What is a proteasome?
A: A large, barrel-shaped macromolecular complex in the cytosol that recognizes ubiquitin-tagged proteins and functions like a garbage disposal to break them down.

Q: What are the primary functions of the proteasome once it recognizes a tagged protein?
A: - Unfolds the protein
- Removes the ubiquitin tags (deubiquitination)
- Cleaves the protein into fragments

Q: What is the final fate of the peptide fragments produced by the proteasome?
A: They are further broken down into amino acids in the cytosol to enter the amino acid pool.

Q: What happens to ubiquitin molecules after the target protein has been processed by the proteasome?
A: They are released and recycled for future use.

Q: What type of enzymes are responsible for protein degradation within lysosomes?
A: Acid hydrolases.

Q: Does the hydrolysis of proteins by lysosomal enzymes require ATP?
A: No, the lysosomal system is ATP-independent.

Q: How does the N-terminal amino acid of a protein influence its degradation rate?
A: The identity of the N-terminal residue affects the protein's half-life; some residues signal for long lives while others signal for rapid degradation.

Q: Contrast the half-lives of proteins with N-terminal serine versus N-terminal aspartate.
A: - N-terminal serine: Long-lived (half-life of more than 20 hours)
- N-terminal aspartate: Short-lived (half-life of approximately 3 minutes)

Q: What are PEST sequences and how do they affect protein degradation?
A: They are specific amino acid sequences rich in proline (P), glutamate (E), serine (S), and threonine (T) that signal for rapid degradation and short intracellular half-lives.

Q: Why is protein degradation considered a non-random process?
A: Degradation is influenced by structural aspects of the protein, such as the nature of the N-terminal residue, the presence of PEST sequences, or chemical alterations like oxidation.

topics.topic: Explain following reactions with enzymes involved in it: 1. Transamination 2. Deamination decarboxylation 3. Deamidation 4. Trans deamination. 5. Oxidative deamination.
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: What is the primary role of the transamination reaction during the initial phase of amino acid catabolism?
A: It functions to channel amino groups from various amino acids onto alpha-ketoglutarate, resulting in the production of glutamate.

Q: Which group of enzymes is responsible for facilitating transamination reactions?
A: Aminotransferases, which are also frequently referred to as transaminases.

Q: What two molecules are produced when an alpha-amino group is transferred to alpha-ketoglutarate during transamination?
A: An alpha-keto acid (the remaining carbon skeleton of the original amino acid) and glutamate.

Q: In which specific parts of the cell are aminotransferases typically located?
A: These enzymes are found within both the mitochondria and the cytosol.

Q: Which two amino acids are unique because they do not participate in transamination during their breakdown?
A: Threonine and lysine.

Q: What essential coenzyme is required by all aminotransferases to function?
A: Pyridoxal phosphate, which is a derivative of vitamin B6.

Q: How does the aminotransferase enzyme physically handle the amino group during the reaction?
A: The enzyme transfers the amino group from the amino acid to the pyridoxal portion of its coenzyme to form pyridoxamine phosphate, which then reacts with an alpha-keto acid.

Q: What is the specific function of Alanine aminotransferase (ALT)?
A: It facilitates the transfer of an amino group from alanine to alpha-ketoglutarate, producing glutamate and pyruvate.

Q: What distinguishes Aspartate aminotransferase (AST) from most other aminotransferases regarding its metabolic direction?
A: Unlike others that funnel nitrogen into glutamate, AST typically transfers amino groups from glutamate to oxaloacetate to produce aspartate for the urea cycle.

Q: Why are transamination reactions considered readily reversible?
A: Because their equilibrium constant is close to one, allowing them to function in both the breakdown of amino acids and the synthesis of nonessential amino acids.

Q: What process is defined as the metabolic coupling of aminotransferase and glutamate dehydrogenase reactions?
A: Transdeamination.

Q: What is the primary outcome of transdeamination in the liver?
A: It allows for the collection of amino groups from various amino acids and their ultimate release as free ammonia.

Q: Which enzyme is responsible for the oxidative deamination of glutamate?
A: Glutamate dehydrogenase.

Q: How does oxidative deamination differ from transamination in terms of its nitrogen product?
A: Transamination moves amino groups between carbon skeletons, while oxidative deamination liberates the amino group as free ammonia (NH3).

Q: Which two coenzymes can be utilized by glutamate dehydrogenase?
A: It can use either NAD+ (typically for the release of ammonia) or NADP+ (typically for the addition of ammonia).

Q: Which two molecules act as allosteric activators and inhibitors of glutamate dehydrogenase?
A: - ADP acts as an activator, signaling low cellular energy.
- GTP acts as an inhibitor, signaling high cellular energy.

Q: What is the primary site in the body for oxidative deamination?
A: It occurs mainly in the mitochondria of the liver and the kidneys.

Q: What occurs during a deamidation reaction involving asparagine?
A: Asparagine is hydrolyzed by the enzyme asparaginase, which releases free ammonia and converts the amino acid into aspartate.

Q: How is glutamine processed via deamidation in the kidneys and intestines?
A: The enzyme glutaminase cleaves glutamine to produce glutamate and free ammonia.

Q: What is the metabolic significance of the deamidation of glutamine in the kidneys?
A: The released ammonia is excreted as ammonium (NH4+), which helps maintain the body's acid-base balance by removing protons.

Q: In the catabolism of branched-chain amino acids, what reaction immediately follows transamination?
A: Oxidative decarboxylation.

Q: Which multienzyme complex is responsible for the oxidative decarboxylation of alpha-keto acids derived from leucine, isoleucine, and valine?
A: The branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase complex.

Q: What coenzymes are required for the oxidative decarboxylation of branched-chain amino acids?
A: - Thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP)
- Lipoic acid
- FAD
- NAD+
- Coenzyme A (CoA)

Q: What medical condition results from a deficiency in the enzymes required for the oxidative decarboxylation of branched-chain amino acids?
A: Maple syrup urine disease (MSUD).

Q: What is the reaction catalyzed by D-amino acid oxidase (DAO)?
A: It performs oxidative deamination on D-amino acids found in the diet, producing alpha-keto acids, ammonia, and hydrogen peroxide.

Q: Which enzyme catalyzes the decarboxylation of histidine to form a potent vasodilator?
A: Histidine decarboxylase, which produces histamine (requires pyridoxal phosphate).

Q: How is serotonin produced from its precursor, 5-hydroxytryptophan?
A: Through a decarboxylation reaction facilitated by aromatic amino acid decarboxylase (also called DOPA-decarboxylase).

Q: What reaction converts 3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine (DOPA) into dopamine?
A: A decarboxylation reaction requiring pyridoxal phosphate (PLP).

learningObjectives.objective: MS-B-007. Transportation of ammonia to liver

topics.topic: Explain the role of pyridoxal phosphate, glutamate, glutamine, alanine and discuss the mechanism of transport of ammonia to liver
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: Which derivative of vitamin B6 serves as the mandatory coenzyme for all aminotransferase enzymes?
A: Pyridoxal phosphate (PLP).

Q: How is pyridoxal phosphate attached to an aminotransferase enzyme?
A: It is covalently attached to the epsilon-amino group of a specific lysine residue located at the enzyme's active site.

Q: During the process of transamination, what intermediate is produced when the amino group is temporarily transferred to the coenzyme?
A: Pyridoxamine phosphate.

Q: What is the general function of pyridoxal phosphate in amino acid catabolism?
A: It facilitates the transfer of an alpha-amino group from an amino acid to an alpha-keto acid.

Q: Besides nitrogen disposal, which rate-limiting step in heme synthesis requires pyridoxal phosphate as a coenzyme?
A: The formation of delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) catalyzed by ALA synthase.

Q: Name three specialized nitrogen-containing products whose synthesis involves pyridoxal phosphate-dependent decarboxylation.
A: - Dopamine (from DOPA)
- Histamine (from histidine)
- Serotonin (from 5-hydroxytryptophan)

Q: Why is glutamate central to the process of nitrogen metabolism?
A: It acts as a collector of amino groups from most other amino acids through transamination reactions.

Q: Which alpha-keto acid acts as the primary acceptor of amino groups to form glutamate?
A: Alpha-ketoglutarate.

Q: What unique reaction does glutamate undergo that allows for the release of nitrogen as free ammonia?
A: Rapid oxidative deamination.

Q: Which enzyme is responsible for the oxidative deamination of glutamate in the liver and kidneys?
A: Glutamate dehydrogenase.

Q: What are the two immediate products of the glutamate dehydrogenase reaction?
A: Alpha-ketoglutarate and free ammonia (NH3).

Q: How does glutamate provide the two nitrogen atoms required for the urea cycle?
A: One nitrogen is provided as free ammonia via oxidative deamination, and the second is provided via the transamination of oxaloacetate to form aspartate.

Q: What molecules act as allosteric regulators of glutamate dehydrogenase to increase amino acid degradation when cellular energy is low?
A: ADP acts as an activator, while GTP acts as an inhibitor.

Q: What is the primary role of glutamine in nitrogen disposal?
A: It serves as a nontoxic storage and transport vehicle for ammonia in the blood.

Q: Which enzyme facilitates the synthesis of glutamine from glutamate and ammonia?
A: Glutamine synthetase.

Q: In which tissues is the synthesis of glutamine particularly vital for removing ammonia?
A: The muscle, liver, and central nervous system (CNS).

Q: Once glutamine reaches the liver, how is the ammonia liberated for urea synthesis?
A: It is hydrolyzed by the enzyme glutaminase to produce glutamate and free ammonia.

Q: Beyond transport, what is the role of glutamine in the kidneys?
A: Renal glutaminase releases ammonia that is excreted as ammonium (NH4+) to help maintain the body's acid-base balance.

Q: What is the source of the pyruvate used by muscle to create alanine for nitrogen transport?
A: Pyruvate is the end product of aerobic glycolysis.

Q: How is alanine formed in skeletal muscle during amino acid catabolism?
A: The amino groups from amino acids are transferred to pyruvate via transamination.

Q: Describe the steps of the glucose-alanine cycle.
A: - Muscle transaminates pyruvate to alanine.
- Alanine travels through the blood to the liver.
- The liver transaminates alanine back to pyruvate.
- The liver uses that pyruvate for gluconeogenesis to create glucose.
- Glucose returns to the muscle to be used for energy.

Q: What is the metabolic fate of the nitrogen carried by alanine once it reaches the liver?
A: It is transferred to alpha-ketoglutarate to form glutamate, which then provides nitrogen for the urea cycle.

Q: Why must ammonia be converted into glutamine or alanine for transport through the blood?
A: Free ammonia is highly neurotoxic to the central nervous system, so circulating levels must be kept extremely low.

Q: Identify the two distinct pathways humans use to move ammonia from peripheral tissues to the liver.
A: - The glutamine synthetase pathway (used by most tissues).
- The glucose-alanine cycle (used primarily by muscle).

Q: What are the primary sources of ammonia in the human body?
A: - Transdeamination of amino acids in the liver.
- Hydrolysis of glutamine by glutaminase in the kidneys and intestines.
- Bacterial action on urea in the intestinal lumen.
- Catabolism of purines, pyrimidines, and amines.

Q: Which enzyme is the major mechanism for ammonia removal specifically within the brain?
A: Glutamine synthetase.

Q: How is the ammonia produced by bacterial urease in the gut handled by the body?
A: It is absorbed into the portal vein and transported to the liver, where it is converted into urea.

Q: What happens to the rate of urea synthesis after eating a protein-rich meal?
A: It increases because higher levels of glutamate and arginine stimulate the production of N-acetylglutamate, which activates the rate-limiting step of the urea cycle.

topics.topic: Illustrate steps of urea cycle with enzymes and its importance
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: What is the primary function of the urea cycle in the human body?
A: It is the most significant metabolic pathway for the elimination of nitrogenous waste from the body.

Q: Which organ is the sole site for the complete synthesis of urea?
A: The liver.

Q: What are the two chemical sources of nitrogen that enter the urea cycle?
A: - Free ammonia (NH3)
- The amino group from the amino acid aspartate

Q: From what molecule are the carbon and oxygen atoms of urea derived?
A: Carbon dioxide (CO2).

Q: Where does urea go after it is produced in the liver?
A: It travels through the bloodstream to the kidneys, where it is filtered and eliminated in the urine.

Q: In which cellular compartments do the urea cycle reactions take place?
A: The first two steps occur inside the mitochondrial matrix, while the remaining steps happen in the cytosol.

Q: What is the first step of the urea cycle and which enzyme facilitates it?
A: The production of carbamoyl phosphate from ammonia and carbon dioxide, catalyzed by carbamoyl phosphate synthetase I (CPS I).

Q: How many high-energy phosphate bonds are consumed during the formation of carbamoyl phosphate?
A: Two ATP molecules are cleaved to drive this reaction.

Q: What specific molecule is required as an allosteric activator for carbamoyl phosphate synthetase I?
A: N-acetylglutamate.

Q: How is citrulline formed in the urea cycle?
A: The enzyme ornithine transcarbamoylase (OTC) transfers the carbamoyl group from carbamoyl phosphate to the amino acid ornithine.

Q: What happens to citrulline after it is produced in the mitochondria?
A: It is moved across the mitochondrial membrane into the cytosol.

Q: Why are citrulline and ornithine not found in body proteins despite being amino acids?
A: There are no genetic codons that correspond to these two specific amino acids.

Q: Which enzyme combines citrulline and aspartate, and what is the resulting product?
A: Argininosuccinate synthetase combines them to create argininosuccinate.

Q: What is the energy requirement for the synthesis of argininosuccinate?
A: It requires the breakdown of one ATP molecule into adenosine monophosphate (AMP) and pyrophosphate (PPi).

Q: Which enzyme is responsible for the breakdown of argininosuccinate, and what are the products?
A: Argininosuccinate lyase cleaves it into arginine and fumarate.

Q: What is the metabolic significance of the fumarate produced during the urea cycle?
A: It can be hydrated to malate, which provides a connection to other pathways like gluconeogenesis or the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle.

Q: How is the actual urea molecule released in the final step of the cycle?
A: The enzyme arginase cleaves the amino acid arginine to produce urea and regenerate ornithine.

Q: What happens to the ornithine that is regenerated at the end of the urea cycle?
A: It is transported back into the mitochondria to participate in another round of the cycle.

Q: Why can only the liver synthesize urea even though other tissues might produce arginine?
A: The enzyme arginase is found almost exclusively in the liver.

Q: What is the total energy cost (in high-energy phosphate bonds) to synthesize one molecule of urea?
A: Four high-energy bonds are used (2 ATP to 2 ADP, and 1 ATP to 1 AMP + PPi).

Q: Why is the urea cycle considered an irreversible process?
A: Because of the large negative change in free energy resulting from the consumption of four high-energy phosphate bonds.

Q: Which reaction serves as the rate-limiting step of the urea cycle?
A: The formation of carbamoyl phosphate by carbamoyl phosphate synthetase I.

Q: How does a protein-rich diet affect the regulation of the urea cycle?
A: It increases the concentration of N-acetylglutamate, which activates the cycle to handle the increased nitrogen load.

Q: What role does arginine play in regulating the urea cycle?
A: Arginine acts as an activator for the synthesis of N-acetylglutamate, which in turn activates the rate-limiting enzyme of the cycle.

Q: What happens to urea that diffuses from the blood into the intestines?
A: It is broken down into CO2 and NH3 by bacterial urease; this ammonia is then either excreted in feces or reabsorbed into the portal blood.

Q: Why is the urea cycle vital for the health of the central nervous system?
A: It prevents hyperammonemia (excess ammonia), which is directly neurotoxic and can cause tremors, slurred speech, brain edema, coma, or death.

Q: What is the common cause of acquired hyperammonemia?
A: Liver diseases, such as viral hepatitis, cirrhosis, or exposure to toxins like alcohol, which impair the liver's ability to process ammonia.

Q: Which urea cycle enzyme deficiency is the most common and how is it inherited?
A: Ornithine transcarbamoylase (OTC) deficiency, which is inherited as an X-linked dominant trait.

Q: How can urea cycle disorders be treated to assist in nitrogen removal?
A: By limiting dietary protein and using medications like phenylbutyrate, which help the body excrete nitrogen through alternative molecules like phenylacetylglutamine.

topics.topic: Discuss ammonia intoxication
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: What is the normal physiological range for ammonia levels in the blood?
A: Healthy concentrations of ammonia in the serum typically fall between 5 and 35 μmol/L.

Q: At what concentration is hyperammonemia considered a medical emergency?
A: When levels exceed 1,000 μmol/L, it becomes a medical emergency because ammonia is directly neurotoxic to the central nervous system.

Q: What are the primary symptoms associated with ammonia intoxication?
A: Symptoms include:
- Tremors
- Speech that is slurred
- Somnolence (excessive sleepiness)
- Vomiting
- Swelling of the brain (cerebral edema)
- Vision that is blurred
- Coma and death at very high levels

Q: What are the two overarching classifications of hyperammonemia?
A: - Acquired hyperammonemia
- Congenital hyperammonemia

Q: What are the most frequent causes of acquired hyperammonemia in adults?
A: Liver disease, which may result from viral hepatitis or exposure to hepatotoxins like alcohol.

Q: How does cirrhosis of the liver contribute to elevated blood ammonia?
A: Cirrhosis can lead to the development of collateral circulation, which causes portal blood to bypass the liver and enter the systemic circulation directly. This prevents the liver from converting ammonia into urea.

Q: What is the primary cause of congenital hyperammonemia?
A: Genetic deficiencies in any of the five enzymes belonging to the urea cycle.

Q: What is the estimated prevalence of congenital urea cycle enzyme deficiencies?
A: These disorders occur in approximately 1 out of every 25,000 live births.

Q: Which specific urea cycle deficiency is the most prevalent?
A: Ornithine transcarbamoylase (OTC) deficiency, which follows an X-linked inheritance pattern.

Q: Why is hyperammonemia resulting from an arginase deficiency typically less severe than other urea cycle defects?
A: Because arginine contains two waste nitrogen atoms and can be directly eliminated through the urine.

Q: What is the quantitative most significant source of ammonia in the body?
A: Amino acids, which are processed in the liver through a combined reaction of aminotransferases and glutamate dehydrogenase (transdeamination).

Q: How do the kidneys contribute to ammonia production?
A: They produce ammonia from glutamine using the enzymes renal glutaminase and glutamate dehydrogenase.

Q: How does intestinal bacteria influence ammonia levels?
A: Bacterial urease in the gut lumen breaks down urea into ammonia and CO2. This ammonia is then absorbed into the portal vein and usually cleared by the liver.

Q: Why do patients with kidney failure often experience hyperammonemia?
A: High blood urea levels lead to increased urea transfer into the gut, where bacterial action produces excessive ammonia that returns to the blood.

Q: How does the brain primarily remove ammonia?
A: By using glutamine synthetase to combine ammonia with glutamate to form glutamine, which serves as a nontoxic transport and storage form.

Q: What is a dietary strategy for treating urea cycle disorders?
A: Restricting the intake of dietary protein while ensuring enough calories are consumed to stop the body from breaking down its own proteins (catabolism).

Q: How does phenylbutyrate help treat ammonia accumulation?
A: It is a prodrug converted to phenylacetate, which binds with glutamine to create phenylacetylglutamine. This new molecule, containing two nitrogen atoms, is then excreted by the kidneys.

Q: What role does neomycin play in managing hyperammonemia in patients with renal failure?
A: It is administered orally to kill the intestinal bacteria that produce ammonia through the breakdown of urea.

Q: Aside from amino acids and bacteria, what are other minor sources of ammonia?
A: - Breakdown of amines (hormones or neurotransmitters) by amine oxidase.
- Catabolism of purines and pyrimidines, which releases amino groups.

Q: Why is ammonia usually found in very low levels in the blood despite constant production?
A: The liver removes it rapidly to form urea, and tissues like muscle package nitrogen as glutamine or alanine for transport rather than releasing free ammonia.

topics.topic: Interpret different types of hyperammonemia on basis of sign symptoms and data
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: What are the normal physiological concentrations of ammonia in the blood?
A: Healthy individuals typically maintain serum ammonia levels between 5 and 35 μmol/L.

Q: What are the common clinical signs and symptoms of ammonia intoxication?
A: Symptoms include tremors, slurred speech, blurred vision, vomiting, somnolence (drowsiness), and cerebral edema.

Q: What are the potential consequences of untreated, high-concentration hyperammonemia?
A: It can lead to coma and death.

Q: How is hyperammonemia classified based on its origin?
A: It is divided into two major types: acquired hyperammonemia and congenital hyperammonemia.

Q: What are the primary causes of acquired hyperammonemia in adults?
A: It is most frequently caused by liver diseases, such as viral hepatitis or damage from hepatotoxins like alcohol.

Q: How does liver cirrhosis contribute to elevated blood ammonia levels?
A: Cirrhosis can result in collateral circulation that shunts portal blood directly into systemic circulation, bypassing the liver and preventing the conversion of ammonia to urea.

Q: What causes congenital hyperammonemia?
A: It is caused by inherited genetic deficiencies in any of the five enzymes involved in the urea cycle.

Q: Which urea cycle enzyme deficiency is the most common?
A: Ornithine transcarbamoylase (OTC) deficiency.

Q: What is the inheritance pattern of Ornithine transcarbamoylase (OTC) deficiency?
A: It is an X-linked disorder, primarily affecting males, though female carriers can also manifest symptoms.

Q: What is the inheritance pattern for urea cycle enzyme deficiencies other than OTC deficiency?
A: They follow an autosomal recessive inheritance pattern.

Q: Why is hyperammonemia in arginase deficiency typically less severe than other urea cycle defects?
A: Because the substrate, arginine, contains two atoms of nitrogen and can be eliminated through the urine.

Q: If laboratory data shows a marked elevation of argininosuccinate in the plasma, which enzyme is likely deficient?
A: Argininosuccinate lyase (also known as argininosuccinase).

Q: Which amino acid is consistently elevated in the blood during hyperammonemia and why?
A: Glutamine levels rise because it acts as a nontoxic storage and transport form that sequesters excess ammonia.

Q: How would a deficiency in the urea cycle affect Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN) data?
A: Urea levels would be decreased because the cycle is unable to effectively synthesize urea from ammonia and aspartate.

Q: What clinical presentation might suggest a urea cycle defect in a neonate?
A: A newborn who appears healthy at birth but becomes lethargic, fails to feed, and develops seizures within the first few days of life (often after 24–48 hours of protein feeding).

Q: How does kidney failure act as a source of ammonia in the body?
A: Elevated blood urea moves into the intestine, where bacterial urease breaks it down into ammonia, which is then reabsorbed into the blood.

Q: What laboratory measurement is used to distinguish between hyperammonemia caused by a urea cycle defect versus other causes?
A: Quantitative plasma amino acid levels can identify the specific intermediate that is accumulating (e.g., argininosuccinate or citrulline).

topics.topic: Discuss the metabolism of aliphatic, aromatic, branched chain, sulfur containing, hydroxyl group containing amino acids with the products formed and enzymes and vitamins involved in them
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: Which metabolic product is formed when the aliphatic amino acid alanine undergoes transamination?
A: Pyruvate.

Q: Which enzyme catalyzes the transfer of the amino group from alanine to alpha-ketoglutarate?
A: Alanine aminotransferase (ALT).

Q: Which vitamin-derived coenzyme is required for the metabolic conversion of alanine to pyruvate?
A: Vitamin B6 (pyridoxal phosphate).

Q: What are the two primary metabolic fates of the aliphatic amino acid glycine?
A: - Conversion to serine
- Oxidation to carbon dioxide (CO2) and ammonia (NH3)

Q: Which enzyme facilitates the interconversion of glycine and serine, and what one-carbon carrier does it require?
A: Serine hydroxymethyltransferase, which requires N5,N10-methylenetetrahydrofolate.

Q: Which enzyme is responsible for the direct conversion of the hydroxyl-containing amino acid serine into pyruvate?
A: Serine dehydratase.

Q: What is the biosynthetic origin of the hydroxyl-containing amino acid serine in the glycolytic pathway?
A: It arises from 3-phosphoglycerate, which is oxidized to 3-phosphopyruvate and then transaminated.

Q: The catabolism of the hydroxyl-containing amino acid threonine can yield which two different products?
A: - Pyruvate
- Alpha-ketobutyrate (which eventually forms succinyl CoA)

Q: Which enzyme initiates the metabolism of the aromatic amino acid phenylalanine, and what does it produce?
A: Phenylalanine hydroxylase, which converts phenylalanine into the amino acid tyrosine.

Q: What specific coenzyme and additional substrate are required for phenylalanine hydroxylase to function?
A: Tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) and molecular oxygen (O2).

Q: What are the final carbon skeleton products of phenylalanine and tyrosine catabolism?
A: Fumarate (glucogenic) and acetoacetate (ketogenic).

Q: Which vitamin-dependent enzyme converts DOPA into dopamine during catecholamine synthesis from tyrosine?
A: DOPA decarboxylase, which requires Vitamin B6 (pyridoxal phosphate).

Q: What enzyme and vitamins/minerals are needed to convert dopamine into norepinephrine?
A: Dopamine beta-hydroxylase, which requires Vitamin C (ascorbate) and Copper.

Q: What methyl donor is required to convert norepinephrine into epinephrine?
A: S-adenosylmethionine (SAM).

Q: Which copper-containing enzyme is responsible for converting tyrosine into melanin?
A: Tyrosinase.

Q: The aromatic amino acid tryptophan is catabolized into which two metabolic intermediates?
A: Alanine and acetoacetyl CoA.

Q: Which aromatic amino acid serves as the precursor for the synthesis of serotonin, melatonin, and niacin?
A: Tryptophan.

Q: What is the first step in the synthesis of serotonin from tryptophan, and what coenzyme is involved?
A: Hydroxylation to 5-hydroxytryptophan by tryptophan hydroxylase, which requires tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4).

Q: Where are the branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs)—valine, leucine, and isoleucine—primarily metabolized?
A: In peripheral tissues, especially the muscle, rather than the liver.

Q: What is the first common step in the catabolism of all three branched-chain amino acids?
A: Transamination to form alpha-keto acids, catalyzed by branched-chain amino acid aminotransferase.

Q: Which vitamin is essential for the transamination of branched-chain amino acids?
A: Vitamin B6 (pyridoxal phosphate).

Q: What multienzyme complex catalyzes the second step (oxidative decarboxylation) of branched-chain amino acid metabolism?
A: Branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase complex.

Q: List the five coenzymes required by the branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase complex.
A: - Thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP)
- Lipoic acid
- FAD (from Riboflavin)
- NAD+ (from Niacin)
- Coenzyme A (from Pantothenic acid)

Q: What are the final metabolic products of the branched-chain amino acid leucine?
A: Acetyl CoA and acetoacetate (exclusively ketogenic).

Q: What are the final metabolic products of the branched-chain amino acid isoleucine?
A: Acetyl CoA and succinyl CoA (both ketogenic and glucogenic).

Q: What is the final metabolic product of the branched-chain amino acid valine?
A: Succinyl CoA (glucogenic).

Q: Which sulfur-containing amino acid is the precursor for S-adenosylmethionine (SAM)?
A: Methionine.

Q: What enzyme converts methionine to S-adenosylmethionine (SAM), and what is required for this reaction?
A: S-adenosylmethionine synthetase, which requires ATP.

Q: After donating its methyl group, S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) is eventually converted into which molecule?
A: Homocysteine.

Q: Which two vitamins are required for the remethylation of homocysteine back into methionine?
A: Vitamin B12 (methylcobalamin) and Folate (as N5-methyltetrahydrofolate).

Q: Which enzyme and vitamin are required for the transsulfuration of homocysteine to cystathionine?
A: Cystathionine beta-synthase and Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine).

Q: What are the two products formed when cystathionine is cleaved by gamma-cystathionase?
A: Cysteine and alpha-ketobutyrate.

Q: What is the ultimate metabolic end product of the sulfur-containing amino acid cysteine?
A: Pyruvate.

Q: Propionyl CoA, derived from the catabolism of methionine, valine, isoleucine, and threonine, requires which two vitamins to enter the TCA cycle as succinyl CoA?
A: Biotin and Vitamin B12 (as 5-deoxyadenosylcobalamin).

Q: What happens to the sulfur-containing amino acid cystine before it can be metabolized?
A: It is reduced to two molecules of cysteine using NADH.

Q: Which amino acid is the source of the high-energy compound creatine phosphate in muscle?
A: Arginine and glycine (with a methyl group from SAM).

Q: Which enzyme converts creatine into creatine phosphate, and what is the phosphate donor?
A: Creatine kinase, using ATP as the donor.

Q: Which molecule is the byproduct of spontaneous creatine phosphate cyclization and is excreted by the kidneys?
A: Creatinine.
